cards.grammarNote: 오늘 (today) and 내일 (tomorrow) are among the most common time words. They appear after the topic/subject: 저는 오늘 바빠요 (I am busy today).
나중에 includes the particle 에 (at/in), which marks time. 지금 (now) often comes right before the verb: 지금 뭐 해요? (What are you doing now?)
아까 refers to something that happened earlier the same day — not yesterday. 아까 뭐 했어요? (What did you do earlier?). Pair with past tense verbs.
Add 에 to mark a time of day: 아침에 (in the morning), 저녁에 (in the evening). 밤 (bam) = night. 낮 (nat) = daytime.
Days of the week end in 요일 (yoil). Use particle 에 to say 'on' a day: 월요일에 (on Monday). Ask: 무슨 요일이에요? (What day is it?)
주말 (jumal) = weekend. 평일 (pyeongil) = weekday. These are handy general terms: 주말에 뭐 해요? (What are you doing this weekend?)

cards.front: "I go to school today." — put it in Korean word order.
cards.grammarNote: Korean word order: [Topic] + [Time] + [Place] + [Verb]. Time comes before the place marker and the verb, not at the end as in English. This pattern is consistent across sentences.
Frequency adverbs come before the verb: 저는 항상 커피를 마셔요 (I always drink coffee). 보통 adn 가끔 work the same way.
Negative frequency words require the negative marker 안 before the verb: 별로 안 먹어요 (I don't eat much), 전혀 안 가요 (I never go / I don't go at all). 전혀 is the strongest negation.
매 (every) combines with time words: 매일 (every day), 매주 (every week), 매달 (every month), 매년 (every year). 매일 운동해요 = I exercise every day.
